coredata.dc:title: 4-hydroxybenzoic acid specific fluorescent sensor based on N,S-doped carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xiaochen
Qi, Haiyan
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Zhao, Ming
Zhang, Jiayu
Luo, Chao
Ran, Maoxia
coredata.dc:description: 4-Hydroxybenzoic acid is one of the important metabolism biochemical markers of the urine in gastric cancer patients. In this work, a fluorescence sensor based on N,S-doped carbon dots conjugated molecular imprinting polymers (N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs) was prepared to detect4-hydroxybenzoic acid. The novel fluorescence sensor in response to 4-hydroxybenzoic acid ranged from 0 to 100 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 1.27 μmol/L. Furthermore, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid demonstrated strong fluorescence quenching capability on our fluorescence sensor, with the fluorescence quenching mechanism involving a combined effects of dynamic quenching and electron transfer. Lastly, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs were applied to analyze 4-hydroxybenzoic acid inhuman urine samples with high accuracy. The findings showed that N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor might have high potential for effectively, selectively, accurately diagnosing gastric cancer patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor
Molecular imprinting polymers
Carbon dots
4-Hydroxybenzoic acid
Gastric cancer biomarkers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images of N,S-CDs. (b) Size distribution of the N,S-CDs.
TEM images of (a) N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, (b) N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs.SEM images of (c) N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, (d) N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs.
(a) XRD pattern of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs. (b) FT-IR spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S -CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N,S -CDs@SiO2@NIPs.
(a)XPS full scan spectrum of N,S-CDs.(b)ElementcontentofN,S-CDs.XPS spectra of C1s (c), O1s (d), N1s (e) and S2p (f) of N,S-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs.(b)ElementcontentofN,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs.XPS spectra of C1s (c), O1s (d), N1s (e) and Si2p (f) of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency comparison of (a)different amounts of N,S-CDs, (b) different molar ratios of template: monomer,(c) different molar ratios of monomer: cross-linker.

(a)UV absorption spectrum of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N, S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs+4-hydroxybenzoic acid; excitation andemissionspectra of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs.(b) Fluorescentdecay curves of the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs +4-hydroxybenzoic acid.
(a) Stability of pH.(b) Salt tolerance. (c) Stability of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs under UV.(d) Effect of different pH on the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor to detect 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.
Selection of (a) N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs concentration, (b)testing environment, (c) optimal response time.

(a) Emission spectra of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs(inset: emission spectra of N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs). (b)The calibration curves of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs for the detection of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.
(a) Selectivity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs. (b) Anti-interference experiments of N, S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs
Detection of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in urine samples.

head.ce:title: 4-hydroxybenzoic acid specific fluorescent sensor based on N,S-doped carbon dots@molecularly imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar, 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar, 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 4-Hydroxybenzoic acid is one of the important metabolism biochemical markers of the urine in gastric cancer patients. In this work, a fluorescence sensor based on N,S-doped carbon dots conjugated molecular imprinting polymers (N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs) was prepared to detect4-hydroxybenzoic acid. The novel fluorescence sensor in response to 4-hydroxybenzoic acid ranged from 0 to 100 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 1.27 μmol/L. Furthermore, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid demonstrated strong fluorescence quenching capability on our fluorescence sensor, with the fluorescence quenching mechanism involving a combined effects of dynamic quenching and electron transfer. Lastly, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs were applied to analyze 4-hydroxybenzoic acid inhuman urine samples with high accuracy. The findings showed that N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor might have high potential for effectively, selectively, accurately diagnosing gastric cancer patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Gastric cancer is a prevalent malignant tumor with high incidence and mortality rates, posing a significant threat to human health and life [1,2].Nearly two-thirds of gastric cancer patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, losing the best opportunity of therapy [3]. So the early detecting and diagnosis of gastric cancer is one of the keys for increasing the cure rate. Cancer biomarkers are able to reflect the tumor occurrence, development, therefore, analyzing gastric cancer biomarkers for early diagnosis is of great significance [4,5].

4-Hydroxybenzoic acid is one of the importantmetabolism biochemical markers of the urine in gastric cancer patients [6].Due tothe matrix interferences in urine samples, making the pre-processing process cumbersome. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a low-cost, highly selective, easy-to-operate, and stable analysis method for the detection of metabolic biomarker 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in gastric cancer patients.

Fluorescence detection, as a convenient and efficient analysis method, has significant advantages in quantitative analysis of trace substances [7–9].Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of fluorescent nanomaterials for its tunable fluorescence properties, excellent water solubility, and outstanding stability, so CDs extensively used in the field of fluorescent analysis [10–15].Currently, carbon dots-based fluorescence sensors have been successfully employed in chemical and biological sensing fields, including anions (e.g. ClO− [16], S2− [17]), metal ions (e.g. Fe3+ [18,19],Hg2+ [20], Co2+ [21]), small molecules (e.g.amino acids [22], antibiotics [23]), and biomacromolecules (e.g.enzymes [24], DNA [25], bacteria [26]). However, real sample analysis may involve trace substances, thus it needs a specific recognition unit to detect sensitively and selectively. Unfortunately, CDs lack selectivity to absorb target molecules. Molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) can discriminate target molecules by recognition cavities which are predetermined on functional groups, shape and size by template molecules [27–32]. The common MIP preparation methods require organic monomers (acrylic acid or acrylates) and an organic solution phase which represent limitations from environmental and biological points of view [33]. And molecularly imprinted polymers suffer from thermal (stable only up to 150 °C) and mechanical stability issues, improper porosity and poor capacity. The sol-gel methodology as a promising alternative to address these limitations allowing the production of sorbents with controlled porosity and higher surface area [34,35]. Thus the combination of molecularly imprinted technology and sol-gel technology can create influential materials with high selectivity, high capacity and high thermal stability. Because of the great selectivity and recognition, when MIPsintegrated with sensing materials as a specific receptor, it can construct a good sensor platform to detect trace substances in complex substrates [36–42]. Zhang et al. successfully prepared a nitrogen-doped carbon dots molecular imprintedfluorescence sensors (N-CDs@MIPs) for detecting the dye genistein in soy products, demonstrating excellent sensitivity and selectivity [43]. Chen et al. developed a green-emitting fluorescencemolecular imprinting probe (N,S-CDs@MIPs) for the detectingTAP in food [44].

In this study, a novel nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe (N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs) was constructed by a simple sol-gel method to detect 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in urine samples. We used citric acid, taurine and ethylenediamine as source to prepare the CDs by hydrothermal method, then efficiently immobilized the CDs and flexibly assembled them to MIPs via 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). The proposed N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor emerged specificity and sensitivity to detect4-hydroxybenzoic acid in urine samples with low detection limits and satisfactory recoveries.

JEM-2100F (Japan Electronics, Japan) was used for the HRTEM. The TEM images and SEM images were visualized using H-7650 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) and S-3400 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). The infrared spectra was recorded by Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (PE, USA).X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was measured by D8 (BRUKER-AXS, Germany). XPS spectra were detected by usingESCALAB250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). UV–visible spectra were observed by using TU-1901 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Puxi, China). The Fluorescence spectrums wererecorded by RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Production Institute, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was measured on the FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

Citric acid, taurine, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, phenol, benzoic acid and 4-nitrobenzoic acidwere purchased from Aladdin Reagent Factory. 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane, tetraethyl orthosilicate, phenylalanine, cysteine, glycine, arginine, serine, threonine, aspartate and histidine were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine, ammonia solution, anhydrous methanol, anhydrous ethanol, formic acid, AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdSO4, CuSO4, FeCl2, HgCl2, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, NiSO4, SrCl2, NaF, NaOH, Na2HPO4, NaNO2, NaNO3, and NaClO2 were bought from Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China).

The N,S-CDs wasprepared throughusing hydrothermal method. After dissolving 0.9606 g of citric acid and 0.6257 g of taurine in 15 mL of ultrapure water, 0.335 mL of ethylenediamine was added and stirred thoroughly. Then mixture was shifted into a 30 mL high-pressure autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 5 h. The cooled suspension solution was filtrated by 0.22 μm membrane, then dialyzed for 48 h. Subsequently the solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C, and named as N,S-CDs.

0.03 g of N,S-CDs was dispersed in 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol, and stirred for 15 min. Then, 400 μL of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and 400 μL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were sequentially added and stirred for 10 and 30 min, respectively. 1 mL of ammonia solution was added as a catalyst, and the compound continued to stir at 35 °C for 12 h. The product was washed 3 times with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water. Finally, the solid was desiccated at 60 °C to obtain N,S-CDs@SiO2.

N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs were prepared by sol-gel surface imprinting method. Firstly, 0.1381 g of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid was dissolved in 40 mL anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 10 min. Thensolution was stirred for 30 min after added 708 μL of APTES.Secondly, the prepared N,S-CDs@SiO2 was added and stirred for 10 min. Thirdly, 1776 μL of TEOS and 1 mL of ammonia were added and then stirred at 35 °C for 12 h. After the reaction product was obtained by centrifugation and then washed three times with absolute alcohol, then was dried. Finally, N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs was washed withacetic acidand methanol (V/V = 1:9) to remove 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, then dried at 60 °C. Meanwhile, N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPswere synthetized according to the same experimental steps as mentioned above, except that the template molecule 4-hydroxybenzoic acid was not added during the synthesis process.

The solutions of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs or N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs (450 mg/L) were produced. Next, 1000 μL of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid standard solution at different concentrations was added to the above mixture. The spectra were conducted using excitation wavelength of 350 nm and emission wavelength of 445 nm. The fluorescence intensity was denoted as F0 and F when absence and presence of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.
Urine from gastric cancer patients was obtained from the Qiqihar cancer hospital. Urine from healthy individuals was obtained from healthy workers in the laboratory.

The urine sample was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and the sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The 5.0 mL of the treated urine sample was measured and diluted to 50 mL with deionized water for subsequent use.

The preparation process of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPswas shown in Fig. 1 . Blue photoluminescence N,S-CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid, taurine, and ethylenediamine as precursors. The fluorescence quantum yield of N,S-CDs was 45.38 %.Subsequently, the N,S-CDs was surface-modified using the Stöber method, which increased the stability of N,S-CDs and improved surface functionalization that accelerated the subsequent polymerization reactions [45]. Then, the N,S-CDs@SiO2 was used as luminouscomponent, and further modified by adding4-hydroxybenzoic acid, APTES (functional monomer), TEOS (cross-linking agent), and catalyst ammonia through the sol-gel method to constructN,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs. Inside, APTEScould provide enough-NH2 surface binding sites for N,S-CDs and 4-hydroxybenzoic acid.After removing the 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs obtained specific recognition site cavity complementation to4-hydroxybenzoic acid, and the intensity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs recovered. So the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor had high selectivity to 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in complex samples.

The morphology of N,S-CDs was characterized using HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . The N,S-CDs was in spherical shape and uniformly dispersed, with average grain diameter of 3.38 ± 0.79 nm. Clear lattice fringes could be observed, and the lattice distance was 0.22 nm which owing to the (100) plane diffraction of the graphite carbon framework [46].

N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs had analogous XRD patterns (Fig. 5 a), that exhibited a characteristic broad diffraction peak at 2θ around 23°–24°, attributed to the (002) diffractions of graphite carbon structure. The broad diffraction peak in the XRD spectrum suggested the formation of amorphous silica [47]. The results illustrated that N,S-CDs had amorphous carbon structures, and proved that the introduction of MIPs had no effect on crystal structure of N,S-CDs.

The surface structures and functional groups of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, and N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs were characterized usingIRspectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 5b. For N,S-CDs, the absorption peak at 3414 cm−1 was corresponded to the stretching vibration of O-H/C-N. The characteristic peak at 2973 cm−1was related to the asymmetric vibration of C-H bonds. A prominent emission peak at 1656 cm−1was corresponded to the in-plane deformation vibration of N-H bonds. Three characteristic peak at 1383 cm−1, 1191 cm−1and 591 cm−1 were owedto the stretching vibration of OSO, C-O and S-C bonds. N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs have exhibited similar FTIR spectra, in whichthe absorption peak at 3407 cm−1 and 1154 cm−1were associated with the stretching vibration of C-N and C-O bonds. The characteristic peak at 2939 cm−1 was related to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C-H bonds. The peak at 1558 cm−1was assigned tothe in-plane bending vibration of N-Hbonds. Two absorption peak at 1053 cm−1and 784 cm−1was attributed to the asymmetric vibration of Si-O-Si bondsand the stretching vibration of Si-C bonds, confirming the successful formation of a silica imprint layer on the surface of N,S-CDs.

Further analysis of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs was performed using XPS. The four characteristic peaks of N,S-CDs were at 530.88, 399.33, 284.8 and 167.42 eV which corresponded to O1s, N1s, C1s and S2p with elemental ratios of 20.57 %, 12.83 %, 65.38 %, and 1.22 %, respectively (Fig. 6 a and b). The C1s peak was fitted with three characteristic peaks at C-C (284.68 eV), C-O/C-N (286.09 eV), and C-S (287.82 eV)(Fig. 6c). O1s spectrum revealed three characteristic peaks at C-O (530.98 eV), O-H (531.85 eV), and SO (532.71 eV) (Fig. 6d). N1s spectrum exhibited two characteristic binding peaks at C-N (399.97 eV) and N-H (401.62 eV) (Fig. 6e). The fitting results of the S2p peak was decomposed into two characteristic absorption peaks at 167.78 and 168.78 eV, indicating the presence of SO and S-C functional groups (Fig. 6f).

The full XPS spectrum of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs exhibited four distinct peaks at531.99 eV, 400.29 eV, 284.8 eV and 102.43 eV, corresponded to O1s, N1s, C1s and Si2p, and the elemental ratios were 33.73 %, 5.07 %, 46.17 % and 15.03 %(Fig. 7 a and b). The XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 7c) could be divided into three characteristic peaks at C-C (283.03 eV), C-O/C-N (284.27 eV) and C-Si (286.8 eV). O1s spectrum shown three characteristic peaks at 529, 530.59, and 531.65 eV, indicating the presence of O-H, C-O, and Si-O bonds (Fig. 7d). N1s spectrumexhibited two characteristic binding peaks at 397.42 eV and 399.42 eV, indicates existence C-N and N-H bonds (Fig. 7e).The Si2p peak revealed two characteristic peaks at 100.81 eV and 101.51 eV, corresponding to Si-O and Si-C functional groups (Fig. 7f) [48]. The XPS characterization results presented above were coherent with the FTIR results, further demonstrating the successful formation of the silica imprint layer and preparation of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs.

Adding different amounts of carbon dots is largely responsible for different internal structures of the prepared polymers. The addition of carbon dots determines the number and spatial composition of the polymer binding sites, which will affect the fluorescence intensity and adsorption performance of the fluorescent nanocomposites. When the amount of other substances was fixed, the addition level of N,S-CDs was optimized through calculating the quenching efficiency of fluorescent sensor. As shown in Fig. 8 a, when the added amount of N,S-CDs was 30 mg, the prepared molecular imprinting sensor had the best quenching efficiency.

The ratio of template molecule, functional monomers and crosslinking agents play crucial role in the rigid structure and adsorption performance of the molecularly imprinted polymers. As shown in Fig. 8b, when the dosage of tetraethyl orthosilicate was 8 mmol and the molar ratio of hydroxybenzoic acid to APTES was 1:3, the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency was achieved. When the ratio of template molecule to functional monomers was fixed (1:3), the added quantity of TEOS was 8 mmol, N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs exhibited the maximumquenching efficiency (Fig. 8c). Therefore, the optimal synthesized proportion was 1 mmol of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, 3 mmol of APTES, and 8 mmol of TEOS.

There was no additional peak after mixing 4-hydroxybenzoic acid with N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, which proved that the binding of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid on the surface of polymer sensor was caused by physical adsorption (Fig. 9 a). And there was no overlap between the UV–Vis spectrum of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid and excitation or emission spectra of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, which suggested that thedetection mechanism was not fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [49]. The template 4-hydroxybenzoic acid could be combined with specific recognition sites of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs by hydrogen bonding, which might cause electron transfer. For future investigation of the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs was 7.53 ns, while after the addition of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid the fluorescence lifetime was 7.26 ns. (Fig. 9b). The result was consistent with the dynamic quenching. Therefore, based on the above analysis, the mechanism of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs in 4-hydroxybenzoic acid detection likely involved both the dynamic quenching and electron transfer [50].

In order to further explore the stability of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, the pH, ionic strength, and UV irradiation were carried out. In the range of pH2∼12, the intensity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPshad no obvious change, and decreased significantly when the pH values were1, 13, and 14 (Fig. 10 a). The results proved that the prepared N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs fluorescence sensor exhibited relatively stable from strong acidic to alkaline conditions, indicating it was suitable for a wide pH application.

Next, we investigated the influence of ionic strength was measured. The intensity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs remained almost unchanged as the NaCl concentration increases from 0 to 1.0 mol/L, demonstrating the excellent salt tolerance of our sensor (Fig. 10b).

The stability of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs under ultraviolet (UV) light was evaluated. When the sensor was continuously radiating under a UV lamp for 120 min, the fluorescence intensity did not show significant changes, indicating that the prepared sensor performed well photobleaching resistance (Fig. 10c).

As an important influencing factor in actual sample detection, pH will directly impact the intensity of the fluorescent sensor. Futher, the effect of different pH on the detection of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid with the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 10d, in the range of pH3∼12, the intensity kept practically unchanged, and decreased significantly when the pH values were1, 2, 13, and 14. The results implicated that within pH3∼12 range, the test results of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid were unaffected, and also indicating the quenching efficiency was basically unchanged.

To determine the best testing conditions, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs concentration, solvent environment and response time were investigated. When the different contents of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs (200–600 mg/L) were used, quenching efficiency increased with increasing N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs concentration and then decreased, and reached its maximums at the concentration of 300 mg/L (Fig. 11 a).

As shown in Fig. 11b, different solvents (water, methanol, methanol:water (V/V = 1:1), ethanol, and ethanol:water (V/V = 1:1))exhibited different influences on the intensity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs, and the maximum quenching efficiency was obtained in aqueous solution.

The optimal response time for detecting 4-hydroxybenzoic acid was tested. The intensity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs rapidly decreased within 1 min and then slowly decreased over a period of time; after 8 min, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged (Fig. 11c). Therefore, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs concentration of 300 mg/L, water, 8 min were chosen as the testing conditions for following experiment.

The molecular imprinting has specific recognition sites for analyte. To further investigate the specificity of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs for 4-hydroxybenzoic acid detection, the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPstowards potential disruptors, for instance metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+,Cu2+,Cd2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Sr2+, K+, Na+, Cl−, ClO2−, F−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, NO3 −), amino acids (phenylalanine, cysteine, glycine, arginine, serine, threonine, aspartate, histidine), and three structurally similar compounds (phenol, benzoic acid, 4-nitrobenzoic acid). As shown in Fig. 14 a, both N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs and N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs showed no response for all analogues, and N,S-CDs@SiO2@NIPs exhibited slight response to4-hydroxybenzoic acid but response of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs was very significant. These results illustrated N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs hadthe specific recognition to 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, the different response was mainly due to specific recognition cavities of N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs. Moreover, considering the complexity of the testing environment in practical sample analysis, the anti-jamming experiments were studied. As shown in Fig. 14b, when interference substances (10 mmol/L) were mixed with the 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid still dominated the changes of intensity, the influence of foreign substances was almost negligible, which implying the prepared N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs exhibited anti-jamming capability for 4-hydroxybenzoic acid detection. All results proved that the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was suitable for the targetdetection of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in complex environments.

To further investigate the viability and practicality of the N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor, we measured 4-hydroxybenzoic acid content in real urine samples of gastric cancer patients and healthy individuals. As shown in Table 1 , the concentration of five people'urine were measured as 13.65 μmol/L, 13.65 μmol/L, 0 μmol/L, 0 μmol/L and1.70 μmol/L μmol/L and the recovery rate was 97.87 %∼113.63 % (RSD<2.89 %). Thus, the results demonstrated that our N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor exhibited excellent accuracy for detecting 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in actual urine samples. Further proofed that the sensor might be used to monitor metabolite contents of gastric cancer patients.

A new type fluorescent sensor (N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs) was successfully created for the specific detection of 4-hydroxybenzoic acid. Our findings evidenced that N,S-CDs@SiO2@MIPs exhibited a robust response to 4-hydroxybenzoic acid with a low LOD of 1.27 μmol/L. In addition, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid concentration in urine samples were successfully detected with high accuracy. Above all, our fluorescent sensor demonstrated their potential to monitor 4-hydroxybenzoic acid in complicated metabolite of gastric cancer patients.

Xiaochen Zhu: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tao Jing: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jun Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ming Zhao: Software, Investigation. Jiayu Zhang: Software. Chao Luo: Software. Maoxia Ran: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors of this manuscript have directly participated in planning, execution, and analysis of this study. The contents of this manuscript are not now under consideration for publication elsewhere and will not be copyrighted, submitted, or published elsewhere while acceptance by Dyes and Pigments.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001).

CA A Cancer J Clin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Joshi SS, Badgwell BD. Current treatment and recent progress in gastric cancer. CA: A Cancer Journal for Clinicians. 2021;71(3):264-279.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paclitaxel may inhibit migration and invasion of gastric cancer cells via nod‐like receptor family pyrin domain‐containing 3/caspase‐1/Gasdermin E mediated pyroptosis pathway
Chem Biol Drug Des

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu Pp, Wu J, Zhang J, Yu Ty, Wang Yb. Paclitaxel may inhibit migration and invasion of gastric cancer cells via nod-like receptor family pyrin domain-containing 3/caspase-1/Gasdermin E mediated pyroptosis pathway. Chemical Biology & Drug Design. 2023;103(1).
